subject: dsa
question: What is an array and how does it differ from a linked list? Explain with a real-world analogy.
keyConcepts: Arrays use contiguous memory - O(1) access by index
Linked lists use nodes with pointers - O(1) insert/delete at known position
Arrays are fixed size; linked lists grow and shrink dynamically
Trade-off: random access (array) vs flexible insertion (linked list)
hintTier1: Think about how you would store a list of items where you know the exact number of items vs. a list that grows and shrinks.
hintTier2: An array stores elements in contiguous memory with fixed size. A linked list stores elements as nodes each pointing to the next. Array = a row of lockers. Linked list = a scavenger hunt.
hintTier3: An array is a fixed-size contiguous block of memory. Access is O(1) by index but insertion/deletion is O(n) - can be slow because other elements may need to shift. A linked list uses nodes with a value and a pointer to the next node. Access is O(n) but insertion/deletion at a known node is O(1).

subject: dsa
question: Explain the concept of a stack. What are its two main operations and what does LIFO mean?
keyConcepts: LIFO - Last In First Out
push(): add element to the top
pop(): remove element from the top
Real uses: undo operations, browser history, function call stack
hintTier1: Think of a physical stack of plates. How do you add and remove plates?
hintTier2: A stack follows Last In First Out (LIFO). The two main operations are push (add to top) and pop (remove from top).
hintTier3: A stack is a linear data structure following LIFO. Operations: push(item) adds to the top, pop() removes from the top, peek() reads the top without removing. Time complexity: O(1) for all three.

subject: dsa
question: What is a queue? How does it differ from a stack? Give one real-world example.
keyConcepts: FIFO - First In First Out
enqueue(): add to rear - dequeue(): remove from front
Opposite of stack: first added is first removed
Real uses: print queue, CPU scheduling, BFS graph traversal
hintTier1: Think about waiting in line at a ticket counter. Who gets served first?
hintTier2: A queue follows FIFO - First In First Out. You enqueue at the rear and dequeue from the front. Unlike a stack (LIFO), the first item added is the first one removed.
hintTier3: A queue is a linear data structure following FIFO. Operations: enqueue (add to rear), dequeue (remove from front), peek (view front). O(1) for all operations. Variants: circular queue, priority queue, deque.

subject: dsa
question: Explain Big O notation. What does O(n), O(1), and O(n^2) mean in terms of algorithm performance?
keyConcepts: Big O describes worst-case time complexity as input n grows
O(1): constant - one operation regardless of input size
O(n): linear - work grows proportionally with input
O(n^2): quadratic - nested loops, grows with square of input
hintTier1: Big O describes how the runtime of an algorithm scales as input size grows. Think about what happens when you double the input.
hintTier2: O(1) = constant time. O(n) = linear - grows proportionally with input. O(n^2) = quadratic - grows with the square of input, typical of nested loops.
hintTier3: Big O describes worst-case time complexity as n approaches infinity. O(1): constant. O(log n): logarithmic. O(n): linear. O(n log n): efficient sorting. O(n^2): quadratic nested loops. O(2^n): exponential.

subject: dsa
question: What is a binary search tree (BST)? What property must every node satisfy? What is the time complexity for search?
keyConcepts: Left subtree: all values smaller than the node
Right subtree: all values larger than the node
Search time: O(log n) balanced, O(n) worst case degenerate
Self-balancing variants: AVL tree, Red-Black tree
hintTier1: Think about how a BST is organized. For any given node, where do smaller values go and where do larger values go?
hintTier2: In a BST, every node's left subtree contains only values less than the node, and the right subtree contains only values greater.
hintTier3: A BST satisfies: all left subtree values less than node, all right subtree values greater. Search is O(h) where h is tree height. Balanced BST: O(log n). Degenerate BST: O(n). AVL and Red-Black trees guarantee O(log n).
